problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy): brief the UN on guidance for handling environmentally displaced persons (EDPs / "climate refugees") — define who counts as an EDP, decide how to relocate them across destination countries while preserving their culture, and design + quantify policies (economic, social, cultural, environmental) with measurable impacts. This team built a three-part deliverable (PLANNING / POLICY / APPLICATION), applied it to the Maldives relocating to Australia, Sri Lanka, and India, and produced concrete numeric prescriptions for the UN.
models_used: Two-state classification scheme (PEWS / EWS) layered on UNHCR's Displaced-Population taxonomy to operationally define who is an EDP and how many people are at risk

Cultural Value Evaluation System (CVS) — a custom multi-indicator scoring framework (8 indicators: written/oral language, historical buildings, performing arts, custom activities, knowledge & practice, handicraft skills, cultural space) scored on Uniqueness / Range / Population criteria, graded into IUCN-Red-List-style endangerment tiers (Critically Endangered → Least Concern)

Destination Country Suitability Index S — composite of 5 indicators GNH, SSI (self-defined), GDP, Culture Pull Index (CPI), Environment Pull Index (EPI), combined via Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) weights S = 0.1461 GNH + 0.2751 SSI + 0.4798 GDP + 0.0340 CPI + 0.0650 EPI
Z-score standardization of indicators before aggregation
AHP with 5x5 pairwise-comparison matrix + consistency check (CI/RI/CR, CR=0.055<0.1, lambda_max=5.2203)

Two-objective (bi-objective) planning for relocation: maximize suitability S and maximize an accessibility index A_ij = 100/((1-D_ij)T_ij), with a hard 0.5%-of-population capacity cap per destination country
Cobb-Douglas utility function u(C,P)=C^a P^(1-a) to linearize the Environment Pull Index
APGAR-score-style 0-5 scoring for climate similarity (5 indicators: summer/winter temp & precipitation, seasonal difference)

Effective (efficient) labor / Cobb-Douglas production model Y_t = A_t K_t^alpha H_t^beta to measure human-capital contribution of EDPs to destination-country GDP growth (CHE = beta * (dH/H)*(Y/dY); Australia beta=0.923, CHE=70.4%)
State-space model for unobservable total factor productivity: ln A_t = gamma + theta ln A_{t-1} + v_t

Optimal time-allocation model under uncertainty from crime economics — expected-utility maximization over 4 employment/detection states, solved via Kuhn-Tucker first-order conditions, showing dt_i*/dw < 0 (more welfare spending reduces illegal-activity time)
Language-extinction demographic model NB = EDP_j (1±rate)^t (UNESCO definition) to project language-user counts and CVS impact
Pollutant re-mixing model for environmental policy: weighted-average PAP and CDE after migration, PAP = PAP_j(1-AR) + PAP_i*AR with Accommodation Rate AR = EDP_j/TP_j
key_techniques: Operational re-definition first: the team converted the vague term 'climate refugee' into two crisp, internationally-anchored states (PEWS/PEWS) so that 'number at risk' became computable — directly answering the problem's definitional demand before modeling

Cross-domain metaphor transplant as the innovation engine: borrowing the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species to grade endangered CULTURE, and the clinical APGAR neonatal score to grade CLIMATE similarity — both made abstract qualities (cultural value, climate fit) quantifiable

Decomposing 'policy impact' into four orthogonal domains (economy/society/culture/environment) and giving each its own sub-model, so a soft policy question produced hard, separable, measurable outputs

Standardize-then-weight pipeline: Z-score normalization to remove dimensional incomparability, then AHP to assign defensible weights, with a consistency test (CR<0.1) to legitimize the subjective pairwise judgments

Closing the loop on culture: the CVS built in Planning is REUSED as the cultural-similarity input to the suitability index AND as the response variable for cultural-policy impact — one artifact serving three roles (cohesion)

Concrete end-to-end application: Maldives -> {Australia 127,200; Sri Lanka 107,200; India 205,600} plus an actionable UN prescription (e.g., 'raise Australia's CO2 standard to 359,462 thousand metric tons'), turning a model into a usable brief

Validation discipline: out-of-sample back-test of the labor model on 2013-2016 Australian data (errors 4.3/2.4/1.4/3.1%) and an AHP consistency check — rare and credibility-building for a policy paper
why_it_won: From a judge's seat, this paper won because it turned a fuzzy, definition-heavy policy prompt into a fully quantified, end-to-end decision system — and did so with memorable interdisciplinary borrowing. Three things stand out. (1) It answered the actual question asked: it DEFINED the EDP population (PEWS/EWS), it DECIDED relocation numbers, and it QUANTIFIED policies into numbers a UN official could act on — most Problem-F teams stop at qualitative discussion. (2) Its signature innovations are sticky and defensible: scoring culture on the IUCN Red List and climate fit on the medical APGAR scale are exactly the kind of cross-disciplinary leap judges reward, and they aren't gimmicks — they map cleanly onto the modeling need. (3) It is unusually cohesive and validated: the Cultural Value System is reused three times, the indicators feed a transparent standardize→AHP→optimize pipeline, and the team actually back-tested a model and ran an AHP consistency check. The Maldives case study with specific allocation counts and a specific emissions-standard recommendation demonstrated real-world usability, which is the core grading criterion for a policy problem.
section_highlights: Summary page is a model of the genre: it states the three-part structure (PLANNING/POLICY/APPLICATION) up front and previews concrete numbers (relocation counts, the 0.825-point CVS-drop result, the 359,462 emissions figure) so a judge sees the payoff before reading the body. Section 4.1 (defining EDPs via PEWS/EWS against the UNHCR taxonomy) is strong because it resolves the problem's definitional ambiguity rigorously instead of hand-waving. Section 4.3 (Suitability Index + bi-objective relocation with the 0.5% capacity cap) is the analytical core and is laid out as clean STEP 1/2/3. Section 5 cleanly partitions policy impact into four domains, each with its own sub-model, which keeps a sprawling question organized. Section 6 (Maldives application) is the high-value payoff with explicit tables and final numbers. Section 7 (testing — AHP consistency + out-of-sample labor-model back-test) gives credibility most policy papers lack. Assumptions are explicit and load-bearing (e.g., 'most nationals stay as EIDPs', 'per-capita emissions unchanged after migration'), and the strengths/weaknesses section is honest. Heavy, consistent use of flowcharts and labeled figures makes the logic skimmable.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many sub-indices are self-invented with hand-set weights and thresholds that aren't empirically justified: e.g., criterion weights 0.4/0.3/0.3, the CSI weights 0.28 HB / 0.39 WL / 0.32 OL, the 0.5% population capacity cap, and the four-level Range/Population bins are asserted rather than derived or sensitivity-tested

GNH formula GNH = (II x UR x IF)/Gini is dimensionally and economically dubious — multiplying inflation by unemployment rate has no clear meaning, and higher unemployment/inflation raising 'happiness' is counterintuitive; it's used uncritically

No sensitivity analysis on the AHP weights or the bi-objective trade-off, so the relocation outcome's robustness to weight choices is unknown (the AHP consistency check validates internal consistency, NOT correctness of the judgments)
The team admits it did not model the global-warming / sea-level process itself, so the time dimension ('how many EDPs, when') is exogenous and unsupported — a real gap for a climate-driven problem

Data integrity issues undercut trust: 'India has a population of 126.6 billion' (should be ~1.3 billion) in Appendix B, and SSI denominators look mis-scaled — sloppy numbers in the application weaken an otherwise quantified brief

The crime-economics time-allocation model is mathematically elaborate but only yields a sign result (welfare up -> illegal time down); it's not parameterized with real data, so its contribution is qualitative despite heavy notation

The bi-objective formulation is stated (maximize S and A) but the actual scalarization / solution method and the EWS-vs-PEWS branching aren't fully worked through in the application — only the PEWS-style S ranking drives the Maldives numbers

Assumptions are strong and convenient (no switching cost between legal/illegal activity; lifestyle/emissions unchanged after migration; only one country in PEWS/EWS at a time), which simplify the math but limit external validity
reusable_patterns: Operationalize-the-vague-term first: when a policy prompt hinges on an undefined population ('refugee', 'at-risk', 'underserved'), invent crisp, citation-anchored categories (here PEWS/EWS on top of UNHCR) so counting becomes possible — definition is itself a deliverable

Cross-domain scoring transplant: take a well-known scoring system from an unrelated field (IUCN Red List, medical APGAR, credit scores) and remap its tiers onto your abstract quantity. This is a high-leverage, judge-pleasing innovation move that's easy to justify

Composite-index pipeline = collect indicators -> Z-score standardize -> AHP-weight -> consistency-check (CR<0.1) -> aggregate. A clean, reusable recipe for any 'rank/choose among options' policy or evaluation problem

Bi-objective relocation/allocation with a hard capacity cap: maximize a desirability index AND an accessibility/cost index, subject to a per-destination quota — a transferable template for resource-distribution problems (refugees, aid, facilities)
Decompose a soft 'impact' question into orthogonal domains (economy/society/culture/environment), one sub-model each, so qualitative policy becomes a set of separable measurable outputs

Reuse one core artifact in multiple roles for cohesion: build an evaluation system once (CVS), then feed it into the selection model AND use it as the impact response variable — signals integration to judges

Earn credibility cheaply: add at least one out-of-sample back-test (predict held-out years, report % error) and one internal-consistency check; even a small validation section sets a policy paper apart
Always land a concrete numeric application + an actionable prescription (specific allocation counts, a specific standard to set) so the model reads as a usable brief, not an abstract framework
Write a summary that previews the three-part structure and the headline numbers up front — judges should see the answer before the derivation
